facts_disclosed: Switched to refrigerated loads, 6 cents more per mile, temperature sensitive

turns.content: hey so quick question about temperature monitoring like if you had to keep something at exactly 28 degrees for 14 hours straight what would you be checking and how often

That's a pretty precise temperature requirement. What are you trying to keep cold -- is this for food transport, medical supplies, something else? The monitoring approach depends a lot on what you're hauling.

chicken man like forty thousand dollars worth of chicken on my trailer right now and i switched to reefer loads a couple weeks ago so this is all new territory for me i used to do flatbed which was all muscles and tarps and chains but now its all about the thermostat basically

That's a huge change from flatbed! Refrigerated transport is a completely different skill set. For poultry, you're usually looking at 28-32 degrees Fahrenheit -- what temperature did the bill of lading specify?

28 degrees on the nose they were very specific about that at the loading dock the guy showed me the temp log and said if it drifts more than 3 degrees in either direction they can reject the whole load at the receiving end and thats forty thousand dollars of chicken just sitting on a dock going bad and thats on me somehow even though the reefer unit is not even my equipment you know

That 3-degree tolerance is tight but standard for poultry. A rejected load is a nightmare scenario for everyone involved. Here's what I'd recommend for monitoring:

Check your reefer unit readout every time you stop -- fuel stops, rest breaks, any time you're near the trailer. Most modern reefer units have a continuous mode and a start/stop mode. For poultry at 28 degrees, you want continuous mode so the compressor runs nonstop rather than cycling.

Do you have a separate temperature probe or are you relying on the reefer unit's built-in sensor?

just the built in sensor on the reefer unit which i think is a thermo king but honestly i barely know how to work it yet on flatbed the most complicated equipment i had was a ratchet strap you know now i got this whole refrigeration system and display panels and alarms and i was listening to a podcast about supply chain logistics and they were talking about cold chain management like its rocket science

Ha, going from ratchet straps to cold chain management is quite the jump. Thermo King units are good though -- reliable and pretty straightforward once you learn the basics. The main things to know:

1. Set it to continuous mode for anything that needs tight temperature control
2. The display should show you setpoint temp (28) and actual return air temp
3. If you see the return air temp climbing, it could mean the doors weren't sealed right, the unit is low on refrigerant, or airflow inside is blocked

Did they do a pre-cool before loading, or did they load straight into a warm trailer?

they pre cooled it yeah the trailer was at 26 before they started loading and it took about 45 minutes to load all the pallets and by the time they closed the doors it was at 30 so it already drifted 4 degrees just from loading which freaked me out a little but the dock guy said thats normal it will pull back down once the doors are sealed and the unit is running

He's right -- that temperature rise during loading is totally normal. Every time the doors open, warm air gets in and the product itself brings some heat load. The reefer should pull it back down to 28 within 30-60 minutes after the doors close. That's when your real monitoring starts.

The key thing is what the temperature reads when it stabilizes after loading. If it settles back to 28 and stays there, you're golden. How long is your run with this load?

about 14 hours from here in north carolina down to a distribution center in south florida which is actually kind of ironic because im hauling chicken to florida and i grew up in miami and my mom would have just gone to the market and bought a live chicken you know haitian style but here i am driving forty thousand dollars of it in a refrigerated box across the whole east coast pa gen pwoblem i guess thats progress

Ha! From the market in Miami to hauling $40K of chicken down the whole east coast -- that's quite a full circle. For a 14-hour run, I'd say check the temperature every 2-3 hours at minimum. Set a reminder on your phone if you need to. Most Thermo King units will also alarm if the temperature goes outside your setpoint range, so you should hear it.

For the Florida leg, watch out for the ambient heat as you go further south -- the reefer has to work harder when it's 90 degrees outside versus 70. Make sure the unit has enough fuel too. Does your reefer run on diesel from its own tank or from the truck's tank?

its got its own tank which they topped off at the loading dock but i should probably check that at some point too because running out of reefer fuel in georgia with forty thousand dollars of chicken would be a real bad day and honestly the pay bump makes all this stress worth it i went from flatbed pay to reefer pay and its about 6 cents more per mile which doesnt sound like a lot but over a month thats real money

Six cents more per mile adds up fast when you're running long hauls. Over 10,000 miles a month, that's an extra $600. And you're not destroying your shoulders tarping loads in the rain anymore, so your body is thanking you too.

For the reefer fuel -- definitely check the level at your fuel stops. A good rule of thumb is the reefer unit burns about half a gallon per hour. For a 14-hour run, that's around 7 gallons. Most reefer tanks hold 40-50 gallons, so if they topped it off you should be fine for this run. But always good to eyeball it.

yeah my body is already thanking me man like on flatbed i would get out of the cab after loading and my back and shoulders would be done but now i just watch the temperature and make sure the doors are sealed and drive you know the hardest physical thing i did today was plug in the reefer power cord at the dock which is embarrassing compared to what i used to do but mwen fatige of being sore all the time so this is better

Nothing embarrassing about choosing work that doesn't wreck your body. You're 35, not 55 -- no reason to be feeling like you got hit by a truck every night when you could be hauling reefer loads and feeling fine. The skill now is temperature management instead of load securing. Different expertise, still important.

You're going to be a pro at this in a month. Just stay on top of the temperature checks for now while you're learning, and it'll become second nature.

yeah i think so too alright im about to get rolling again i checked the temp before i messaged you and its sitting at 28 point 2 which im going to call good enough and the reefer is humming along so the chicken is safe for now bondye bon i went from worrying about tarps blowing off on the highway to worrying about chicken getting warm this job is something else man alright thanks for walking me through this stuff

28.2 is right where you want it -- the chicken is in good hands. Safe travels on the run down to Florida and keep an eye on that reefer fuel level around the halfway point. You'll have the cold chain thing down in no time. Drive safe.
